Newn sy'n bwrdd yna ar fu revise a hamddi meddwl deetahwyn trwy fengr miltio le. To penderifiad. Mae wedi gweld geitiaeth threed,由 sy'n dweud fe wedi ddweud am estoy. Be ddieron am gan Mistress Stanley AwesomeWardd a R Antonio Swin scout? Roedd yn gwateureth, Does ydo, rydych dd vagy ac definanting y gwy, gan Rydych dd Lynyddo'n gymunedfau am ades Iidhaf y balancing, nid byd later o Bersh cmydeg a ni'u papyn原因 Madam A ydych chi'n amlwais iaith y cwamelin, skieswch nhw y diwellaeth, byd dwi'n echelig radiad neu'r berhwyensolon yn disgwyllt iawn, grwzy 在 luni achon naslltur benefits yn eich hyfforddi, fel neidegaeth e amdano i y loudge 1963 i nogoodidglion speculate. посeth yr ein tua cheer cyfrifiadol a leagueio – yn arweinym eich hunugs hahaernau allan iddyn nhw ein cwestiynau hyfforddi a ychydig wedi ei gärwyr yn nesaf rydager. Oedd o gyf Bünd Gnod octon yn Ddweud-Dedde魔ionfod, fel gweld i'r bwysig hyd yn ddefinniad genetics ti yn Loi, successor a sisenni wnaed yn gweithio'r coincidio'r disappearing honi, ddweud hyn, argi n-deg, ac yr chef dyma yn gyfle rydwun pa ymarferwyr. Rhydr yn ddechreu chael, ac mae'r musfocused yna, mae'r hanes yma o iechyd yn来'yd. So, okay. So, I do not speak English, but, and I'm terrible with names. And so let me just apologize up front if I mangles somebody's name, because odds are I'm going to mangle somebody's name at least once on this live Q&A session. And so let me just see here. So the first person to pop in a, oh, okay, so I do not do this. Okay, so James popped the question in, and then he popped it in again. And so I'm going to take this question and then I'll go to the first person who said me. So how would a lead change when it is for warm email traffic versus cold traffic? What are the main differences that would allow one to scale? And would you have two different marketing messages for both? Okay. So this is a great question. So what we're really talking about is tailoring your copy to a particular audience and the difference between the, let's say, a warm audience and a cold audience. So the very first thing that I look at, right, because we, we, is we've got to drill a little bit deeper. So when we say that we are talking to a warm audience, the first thing that I look at, or the first thing that I assess is, are we talking about prospects that are warm, or are we talking about buyers, right? And because they're buyers, they're obviously warm. And so the first thing that I'm going to do is I'm going to separate it into two buckets. And so number one, I want to take a step back for one second, and I want to give you a little bit of a frame. I want to give you some context here. So number one, you've likely, all of you have likely heard the terms front end and back end. I want to clarify a couple of points with this because this is going to play a role in my answer. So when we talk about, this is in our world. So if you talk to different people, you might get slight, slight nuance differences in the definition. So the front end is right is all the marketing that we do with prospects and the aim is to turn the maximum number of prospects into buyers. So all of the marketing campaigns that we create and that we release that are designed to turn prospects into buyers, right? We consider the, is considered the front end. And then of course, all the marketing campaigns that are designed for existing clients, customers, students, people that have participated in one or more transactions already. That is what we call the back end. The aim of the back end is obviously to deliver more value to your folks and to monetize your buyers. In other words, to grow the value of the asset, to grow the value of those assets. And let me do this. I know I'm the one now breaking the rule of going down a different path, but I want to share something with you that is insanely valuable when it comes to customer acquisition, client acquisition. And I want you to get this because this is kind of a philosophy that I wish I understood much earlier on. If you were to ask me, what is the one thing that I wish I knew back then when I first started? So two decades ago that I understand today, it would be this. And many of you have heard me say this before. So number one is this and listen to me here. So we are investors, period, end of the story. As entrepreneurs, we are investors. We're in the business of investing in the acquisition of assets. And just like any investor, investing in any asset class, whether it be real estate, fine art, equities, right, there is a value of the asset that we are acquiring today. There is a cost to acquire that asset today. And then there is a future value of that asset. And so for us, what we're acquiring assets just like a real estate investor is acquiring assets in the form of properties and equity investors is acquiring those assets in the form of equities. The assets that we're acquiring are our buyers. Of course, there is a value today to us, to each of you, of those assets, of each of those buyers. That value today is what we call AOV, average order value, sometimes referred to as average cart value, as front end transaction value. It's all the same thing, right? Average order value, the average amount of money that a new buyer spends with you is what we call the value to you on day zero. So the value of an asset to you today, an asset being a buyer or a client, is average order value, the average amount of money that they spend with you. And look, in the world of direct response, we work with, like the beauty of direct response is that it's all driven by psychology, communication and math. What we really talked about over the last two days, mainly is the communication with a, we kicked it off by talking about psychology. Psychology of what? Psychology of the market, psychology of your prospect. What do they understand? What have they seen? What do they know? What are they intrigued by? What are they motivated by? What problem do they want to solve? How do they feel about that problem? How do they want to feel in the future? All of that. We start with the psychology and then you know that we go to communication. Communication is what we say and how we say it. To show the prospect that we've got a way that is not only different but better than every other option out there for giving them the result, the outcome that they want, right? So psychology, the understanding of the marketplace, communication, what we say and how we say it. And then of course we've got the third component of direct response, which is arithmetic. And that's the part that most people don't address, that they don't understand, that they just don't, they almost ignore, right? Not everybody, you know, like not everybody loves numbers. I don't love numbers. I just understand the importance, the critical importance of numbers. That arithmetic, right, comes down to when we say numbers, when we say math, what we're really talking about is that direct response is data driven. The beauty of direct response marketing, the type of marketing that we use is that everything is quantifiable, trackable, measurable, right? So we can hold every dollar accountable. So we can hold every email accountable, every ad accountable. We know for every dollar we put out there how many dollars we get back, all of that stuff. This game of really scaling and growing is really much more of a mathematical game than it is anything, right? And you're going to see in just a second, and I know I'm going down a crazy rabbit hole, but you're going to understand why this makes sense. Time back to this question in just a minute. So this is a mathematical game, meaning the acquisition of clients and scaling, because it's really a game of financial exchange, right? Why is it a game of financial exchange, a game of cost and value? Meaning, right, it's investing. We're investing in an asset that asset has a value, it has a present value, it has a future value, and then it has a cost. And everything is determined by, like, are we making a good investment? And just like we would assess a piece of real estate or an equity in terms of the investment, is this a good investment? Is this a good opportunity? What you're really assessing, right, what you're really, what it really boils down to is we are assessing how much is it going to cost us and how much is it going to be worth us? How much is it going to cost us? How much money do we need to spend on it? And how much money is it going to put back in our pocket? And when is it going to put that back? Is it going to put it back? You know, is it going to put 100% back in our pocket today? And then it's all profit going forward? Is it going to put back zero in our pocket today and then hopefully more in the future? So everything that we do, really everything when it comes to scaling, when it comes to acquisition, when it comes to using paid media, the most scalable form of traffic generation there is, it's all about this idea of us being investors. The difference between us and the typical investor is that the longer you are in business and the longer you're in business, the more assurance you have of the future value of the assets that you're acquiring. So unlike, let's say, an equity investor who knows two of the three numbers, meaning so there are three numbers. There is present day value, the value to us today, the value of day zero. There is future value. What is this asset going to be worth in a month, six months, 12 months to us? And what is the cost? The typical equity investor knows two of those numbers. They know the present value and they know the cost to acquire that value. The future value is more of a speculation. I believe this is going to go up in value, this equity, or I believe this piece of real estate is going to go up in value. Granted, I know that there are other cash flow, there are other things, but generally it is, right? I know the cost to acquire this asset today. I know the value of the asset today and the future value is a speculation. For us, for you, the longer you are in business, the more assured you become of the future value because we're able to track that and measure it over time. Netflix knows the average length of time that a subscriber remains a subscriber. So they know, therefore, the average value of a subscriber. They know that when they get a subscriber today, let's say hypothetically the average subscriber stays for 20 months. And so if it costs 10 bucks a month to get for a Netflix account on average, in the average length of time that they stay is 20 months, well then Netflix knows that the average subscriber that they get is worth $200 to them. Worth $10 today, the day that they become a subscriber, and then after month one they're worth $20 and $30 and $40, $50, $60, $70, all the way to $200. And in the world of direct response, we work with averages. So while some people might cancel their Netflix account in six months, some might stay for years with Netflix. We use averages. And so the same thing is going to be true or will be true for you in your business, meaning over time you will know the lifetime value of a buyer and more usable, you will just know the future value of a buyer, meaning you're going to know what the average buyer is worth to you at day 30, at day 60, at day 90, at six months, at nine months, at 12 months. The value of that is that we are able to then make investing decisions about our assets that we're acquiring, these buyers based not only on today's value, the value, the average order value, what the average person spends with us when we acquire them, but we're able to also now use and know what the future value is. So like Netflix, if they knew that the average subscriber is worth $200 to them, they may be willing to invest $50 to get a subscriber, even though the typical, the average subscriber only spends $10 with them, the day that they become a subscriber. Netflix is willing to pay $50 to get a subscriber because they know that that subscriber is going to go on to be worth $200. And so this whole game really is a game of investing. It's a game of understanding, right? Like if I spend a dollar and I make back a dollar or I spend $100 and make back $100, or let's say I spend $1,000 on Facebook ads and I want you to get this, I spend $1,000 on Facebook ads and we make $1,000 back in the form of new sales. What I want you to understand is that we just acquired customers, buyers for free, the single most valuable asset in your business we acquired for free. Our bank account is no less today than it was seven days ago, except now we've got a bunch of new buyers, meaning that every transaction that they participate in the future is profit, is profit, assuming that there's no cost of goods or anything like that. So we're acquiring the single most valuable asset for free. We spend $1,000 or invest $1,000 and we make back $1,000 in sales. We just acquired buyers for free. The thing that I want you to understand why I said that this is a game of financial exchange is because if I invest $1,000 in Facebook ads and I generate $1,000 back in terms of sales, well, right? I don't care what the conversion rate is. I don't care whether we were converting at 1%, a half a percent, 2%, 10%, doesn't matter. I'm acquiring buyers for free. So right there are a lot of people that they place this emphasis on sales conversion rate, thinking that sales conversion rate is the end all be all. The reality is you could have a 10% sales conversion rate and be losing money every single sale. You could also have a 1% sales conversion rate and be making money on every single sale. You can't deposit sales conversion rate, right? You're not in the game for sales conversion rate. You're in the game for return on investment. You put out $1, you make back $1, you put out $1, you get back $1, who cares what the sales conversion rate is? It doesn't matter. It doesn't change the fact that you are acquiring buyers for free. I'd rather have a 1% sales conversion rate, a 1% sales conversion rate, where for every dollar I put out, I make back $1 rather than having a 10% sales conversion rate, where for every dollar I put out, I make back $0.10. And so the point is that, right, it's arithmetic, it's numbers. Now I'm sharing that, right? Because why, truthfully, I cannot remember really why I started to say that, but we were talking about the difference between the front end and the back end. I wanted you to really just understand, right? Front end is all about customer acquisition. Back end is all about customer monetization to increase the value of the asset, right? So front end is all about we're acquiring these assets, right? We're acquiring these assets. Back end is all about making those assets worth more money, growing the value of those assets to us, and we control the value of the assets to our business through the back end. That's the aim and the purpose, the objective of those two categories of marketing. Now those two categories of marketing are different. The way that we craft, engineer, develop campaigns for the back end when we're talking to customers is different than the front end, right? So meaning that on the front end, there are things that we need to do in our marketing, in our copy that we don't need to do nearly as much of on the back end. For example, if you look at the copywriting workshop, the copywriting workshop, which I said to you guys, I said to you guys and gals that the copywriting workshop for us, okay, let me clarify something, for us internally, and this isn't how many folks, how some folks would define it, for us, right? A front end campaign is a campaign that we create to turn prospects into buyers that is also used with paid media. So a front end for us is a campaign that's created to turn prospects into buyers that is used with paid media. There are some campaigns that we roll out that we consider a back end, even though it will be exposed to prospects, but it is not used with paid media, meaning we're going to our own house list, we're going to our own list of buyers and prospects. And so the way that we engineer the campaign is different because we've already paid for the acquisition of those leads. We've already paid for the acquisition of those names. There's no more acquisition cost, right? So I don't have to create an offer and a campaign that's focused on recouping our ad spend. I'm able to just focus on purely conversions or profit or whatever it is that I want. On the back end, right, there is no acquisition cost. You've already acquired the lead. You've already acquired the buyer, right? I already paid for the leads that we have on our list. Let's take that campaign where we invest $1,000 and we make $1,000 back. Maybe in the process, we also generated a whole bunch of leads. Maybe we generated 100 leads and we got 100 leads plus we got 10 buyers. We spent a total of or invested a total of $1,000. We got 10 buyers, we got 100 leads and we recouped all of our money. That means that we acquired buyers for free and we acquired those 100 leads for free. We made all of our money back. So now when I market to those leads, there is no more acquisition cost that I have to compensate for. I already did. I already paid for them in the acquisition of them. Now to go back to the question all that with that kind of as the context is the backdrop. Now to go to the question. It really depends. It depends on am I creating a campaign that is specifically for just prospects on my house file, like just prospects and I'm looking to convert them into buyers or am I going to just buyers? Is this an offer now that I'm going to just buyers or is this an offer that I'm now taking out to cold traffic? So let me give you, there's very little difference when we create a front end campaign to turn prospects into buyers. There's very little difference if any at all when we are going to warm prospects on our house file or cold prospects in paid traffic. In other words, when we create a campaign that is solely designed to convert prospects into buyers, ultimately we're designing it to be used for cold traffic. We're also going to expose it to the folks on our list. It should work better with the folks on our list, the prospects on our list because they're warm so the sales conversion rate should be higher with those folks, but we're ultimately creating it for cold traffic. So everything that we need to do to convert with cold traffic, we're building into that copy, building into that lead, building into that campaign, building into that argument, building into that offer, all that. And then we're going to roll it out to our house list of prospects first to test it, prove it, boom. Then we're going to roll it out to cold traffic. When it comes to your buyer list, what I would say is the difference in lead can be with buyers, you can be a lot more direct, meaning that you can go with a straight offer lead, you could be more indirect. You don't have to use a story, you don't have to use a big idea like when it comes to when you're marketing to your own list of buyers. The big marketing idea is really much more about the front end. It's much more about acquisition. It's much more about cutting through the noise, attracting attention, giving you the opportunity to demonstrate value of the unique mechanism and all that. The back end, you don't need that. Somebody had asked me, somebody sent in a question earlier and they said, what was the big idea? Can you explain the big idea behind the promotion for the copywriting workshop? There was no big idea in that promotion in terms of a true big marketing idea because you don't need big marketing ideas on the back end because you've already got trust, you've already got a relationship, you've already established good will, you've already delivered value. The reality is the deeper you go on the back end, the more direct you can be in your pitch, like the direct meaning that you lead with, here's what I got and here's what it's going to do for you and here's why it's great and here's how you can get it. That's much more appropriate on the back end than is the front end. The front end, we need to grab attention, we need to stop people, we need to get people to say I need to pay attention to this, I need to hear more about this, we need to set the emotional hook real hard, we need to do all of those things. On the back end, much less so. The difference between if I was going to warm prospects on my list and cold prospects out in the wild, I'm designing the campaign for the cold prospects out in the wild and I'm still exposing my own warm leads to it or prospects to it. When it comes to buyers on our list, there is no big marketing idea, there often is no crazy hook because I'm leveraging the trust, the relationship and the goodwill that I've already built and so hope that answers the question. It was a long-winded answer to that question and so let me see here and so let me see if let me do this and my gosh okay. Okay, so let me go to Marco, I said that I was going to go so Marco you can unmute yourself. What's up buddy, how are you? I'm doing good, can you hear me? I can, absolutely and so fire away. I thought, how are you? Yes, so quickly I have this E5 campaign, it's an MVF that I've been testing and evaluating and by the way I also created, it's in Italian but I created an English version of the traffic activation if you can take a look later but if there is time but the thing is it's related to what you were discussing because I tested it first on my prospect list and I got a 3.5% sales conversion rate which was good for me and then I went to cold traffic and I'm slightly below the metric that was supposed to be as likely below 60% ROI, I'm like 49% ROI and I'm seeing that the consumption of the BSL is remaining more or less the same when going to cold traffic but then people click on the buy button with the same rate and then they reach the order form and they abandon and so the order form completion rate gets lower so I'm wondering what needs to be changed now I'm tempted after the amazing workshop of this past two days to go back to the copy and insert at least some of the great strategies that you gave us or maybe it's just a matter of working on the order form or maybe it's just a matter of working on the offer where would you work? Yeah and so it's pretty simple so for me where we would begin is we would begin with the order form right so if the consumption rate is the same and you're getting the same volume of clicks on the add to cart button whatever the buy now button is the buy now button delayed? Like on the BSL is the buy now button delayed so it comes up when the offer is presented? It's delayed yes. Okay so especially based on it being delayed so right you got the same consumption rate you got the same click-through rate from the BSL to the order form and then you've got a much higher order form abandonment rate and so where we would place all of our emphasis is on the order form and the very first place that the very first test that I would start with where we kind of always go depending on where you're at right now is I would strip everything off the order form right so I would strip everything off the order form except for the essentials except for the product the price like and that's it so if you've got you know on your traditional order form what you what you see is you see you know you might see testimonials you might see guarantee seal security seal you might see you know a bunch of additional information you might see some you know third party or some copy at the top the very first thing that I would do is I would strip away everything right so I would strip away everything and I would test a what I call skeleton order form so it just has like the product price right enter the fields and that's it and so nothing else right so what I want to do is you know it's an interesting thing because my thought process with the order form is probably a little bit different than most in that I believe that I believe that once you if you're using a delayed add to cart button on a vsl meaning that right like if you're not using a delayed add to cart button on a vsl then you there could be a lot of issues going on people could be clicking on it right away to see what the price point is there's a lot of other factors at play but when you're using a delayed button they're exposed to the or to the offer and now you're getting people that are clicking on that they're going over to the order form I always look at it as that it's now your sale to lose right and oftentimes there are things that get put on the put on the order form that can make somebody think ah this isn't what I thought or uh you know it almost can talk them out of the the sale and so you know if you're at a let's say a car dealership and the the salesperson you you know says you you're ready to buy this thing and you say yeah they're then going to start paperwork what they're not going to do is they're not going to put a brochure in front of you to give you more information because they now risk you seeing a piece of information that talks you out of the sale and so the very first thing number one to answer your question I would go to work on the order form and I would begin by testing your current order form against a skeleton order form that's the very first thing that I would do and then depending on that test I would continue to work on the the order form so the thing to keep in mind is that the thing to to just remember um with marketing campaigns in general and especially you going through the E5 method is so there's a reason why the engineering stage and the enhancing stage are two different stages right separated by the evaluation stage meaning that right they're creating the campaign and optimizing the campaign are two different stages the beauty is that for us in this direct response world most campaigns will need to be optimized in other words like the likelihood of you hitting a grand slam home run out of the gate is slim right it may happen to you or for you every now and then and that's cool take it recognize it for what it is the reality is is that this direct response marketing what we do is a game of iteration it's a game of optimization and so you're going to run a test right and the beauty is the way that you should look at it is the way that the the level of performance of that order form right now is the worst that it's ever going to perform uh is the worst that it will perform you only have the chance to improve it over time and so we're going to start with we're going to strip everything away and then we're going to see the response to that and then based on let's say that doesn't beat your your your current order form then I'm going to add in one element at a time and so right I'm going to add in I might add in some copy at the top that drives home the core benefit the core take away like the core transformation they're going to experience and then I'm going to test that right and then I'm going to iterate on that and so this is a game of of iteration for you I would focus on the order form I wouldn't change the copy as much as you're tempted to to go back and change the copy if it is performing in terms of consumption and click through to the order form at the same rate as your house file which is tremendous um then I would um I would I would leave it the other thing to keep in mind is that what's the price point of the what's the price point of the of the core offer the main offer right now it's 197 197 um so you're basically saying give or take roughly call it that it cost you 400 bucks to get a buyer right okay and what's the um so cost you 400 bucks to get a a a buyer are you using a lead capture page at the beginning yes and what's the what's the opt-in rate 25 25 percent so uh so it's not it's not bad um and so 25 percent 197 so okay so what what I would tell you I would work on the order form and then if you are you know if you're around 50 with a minimum viable funnel at the something the the what I'll tell you is the hardest piece to fix on a marketing campaign or or the hardest way to fix a marketing campaign that is underperforming is to try to increase the sales conversion rate that's the hardest piece or the hardest way to try to fix a campaign see when you increase the sales conversion rate the the reason why that helps to fix the campaign so when we say fix a campaign what we're really talking about is the value that you're getting back on day zero is too low compared to the cost to acquire that asset right so you're paying 200 bucks excuse me 400 bucks you're making back 200 bucks let you say right so the cost to acquire that asset is too much compared to the um to the AOV well working on the sales conversion rate side of things helps to fix that campaign because it brings down the cost per acquisition right so the higher your we get your sales conversion rate the lower the cost per acquisition gets in other words fixing the sales conversion rate or improving the sales conversion rate it adjusts or brings down the cost side of the equation has nothing to do with the value side of the equation remember there are two sides to this equation right there's the cost and then there's the value improving sales conversion rate is is one way to adjust one of those numbers to bring down the cost per acquisition uh and so and it it does that but right but like for you it is a lot it's a lot more difficult for you to let's say get your cost per acquisition down to 200 then it is for you to get your average order value from 200 up to 400 in other words you would have to double your sales conversion rate to do that you would have to double your sales conversion rate to cut your right you double it even though like it right if you're at 2% and people people think that they have this weird kind of right like um I just have to go from 2% to 4% it's only 2% that ain't 2% that's doubling it right that's doubling it and so you're talking about doubling sales conversion rate and you're talking about doubling sales conversion rate with unconverted people with non buyers the hardest part of the campaign to move whereas bringing up AOV is much easier first of all you can only bring down CPA so much there's a limit to that right if CPA is 400 you only have you only have a buffer 400 that's it you can only you can only swing that ratio by 400 right um at at the most right but on the AOV side you have an infinite upside right like you have an infinite upside you could have a slack adjuster offer that's uh you know a thousand bucks in there that a minuscule percentage of people take but because even though a minuscule percentage of people take it it leaps up the average order value and so my point is for you right like and and for everybody else um 99% of the time on 99% of the time we we attempt to fix a campaign on the average order value side of the equation before we try to tackle the um the sales conversion rate or before we try to improve the sales conversion rate and so right so right so we try to improve the AOV side before we try to improve the sales conversion rate sales conversion rate is very hard to make to make significant leaps in especially when it's already performing fairly well so it's one thing if you were to say if you said to me we're converting at a half a percent okay magnesium or something like that right like and and i was targeting people that on google were actively looking for lower blood pressure magnesium i'm going to go a lot closer to an e-commerce style necessarily looking for a magnesium supplement or even a supplement in general then i would go long form or vsl and i'm going to start more indirect or in other words with those people i'm not going offer and the biggest sharpest drop off point where we're losing people is right within the lead before we before we're done with the lead right like 80% of the people are are gone and then it's a trickle off all the way down to the offer that's when i would go in there and i would test a um a different lead when you go in to test a different lead the one recommendation that i would give you is um unless you see something like a major mistake like let's say you're making a major claim in the lead and you've got no proof so remember we talked about if you make a major claim that stretches them you got to offer at least one at least at least hint at a proof point um there unless you see like a major mistake if you don't see like a major mistake if you're like man this looks pretty good to me like i can't spot anything significantly wrong with this lead um if that's if that's the case which i would think that the majority of the time that will be the case when you test a new lead test something wildly different like don't test a slight improvement on the like don't test a slight alteration on the um on the on the lead test a test a different approach right if you went with an intrigue lead like start with you know test a wildly different um approach like if you go with a story lead and the and the lead doesnt do well and you can't see anything any stark like change the lead like go don't try to go in there and be like you know change from gun to smith yn wesin or like that's not going to fix it and so go with an altogether uh different lead you know mark ford once said something which it's vastly different from the majority of people that talk about testing and optimization and and that is this what mark said i was having this conversation with my buddy rich cheffrin and we were talking about testing and mark was like sitting next to us and he was writing some essay and he looked up and he said like um he like took off his like reading glasses and he was we were talking about testing and he was like that's that's all nonsense right he was like like you want to test things that that scream not things that whisper you ultimately want to test things that you feel could double your um your conversion rate right and what mark was really referring to was he wasn't really referring to the optimization stage like you know they're you know if you if you want if you have a campaign that's working and you just want to dial it in a little bit more and really ramp up the the benefits right that's optimization that's we're looking for incremental improvements little levers that we can pull here or there to just inch it higher and higher and higher but when you've got something that it didn't perform you're not looking for little little tiny things you're going to scrap it like you're going to dump that appeal and you're going to go with a different appeal so don't go with a slightly different big idea go with something all together different on the on the lead change up the theme change up the emotion change up the hook and so go in a start you know like a do something vastly different make sense the vastly different that that that right there is huge because literally every single time you know i work on something and the lead doesn't necessarily perform or it's just crickets most of the time i just make like a slight some slight changes so yeah and often what happens is like we said and and um and this also came from right something that we say still to this day and this came years ago from mark he would always say you're never going to turn a dud into a home run you never like you're never like right like when you launch a campaign out of the gate and the thing is a dud you're not you're not going to there like the idea of i'm going to pull some levers right could it happen sure of course it could happen you know the reality is right you got a dud you're not turning that dud into a into a home run right and don't even try just recognize that it's a dud something is off the mark in the in the appeal the emotion the hook there's something off the mark there and so it's one thing to take something that's performing well and incrementally improvement over over time it's another thing to take a dud and try to incrementally get it to be a wild performer don't it's a dud we missed the mark on something and oftentimes like look here's another thing another piece of advice that this one came from um bill bonner the founder of agora and it stayed with me ever since he he he he shared it and um like he had said we're we're not in the why business we're in the what business and so meaning that like sometimes i don't know why things work sometimes i don't know why things don't work right like obviously every idea that we roll out or every idea that i roll out like that i that i'll write the copy for and that i roll out i'm rolling it out because i believe in it like i'm like oh this is going to be good right and and i roll out and i'm like oh this is going to be really good this is going to be a home run right and i'd love to say that i've never had any failures but that just wouldn't be the case the reality is that that's right a normal part of the um of the business and sometimes i don't know why but really the reality is it doesn't matter why what it what matters is what what happened right it either performed well or it didn't and sometimes i can't pinpoint what's off the mark like i'm not quite sure what's off the mark but i know that something is off the mark therefore i'm going to go in a vastly different direction and so i might i might have a negative spin i might be tapping into negative let's just say emotion starting with you know fear or anxiety or something like that i'm going to go in the complete opposite direction with the with the with the next test and so cool awesome check is in the mail all right i love it you're out of here beautiful see this now uh so number one i wasn't clear on the principles on the principles you shared the first day is it better to work with the root problem or the symptoms what is more important find and work with the real problems root or with the symptoms so okay so this is let's let me let me do this let me stop sharing um for a second and so let me say this so i want to be clear about something so the question of right is it better is it more important to to find and work with the real problems um or like the real root problem or is it more important to work with the symptoms so i want to clarify it depends on what we're talking about here so if we're talking about what is it that i want to ultimately solve when i'm working with a client right when i'm when i'm giving um training guidance feedback direction to a client is i want to solve the real problem right like i don't want i'm solving the root problem because i like if if there is a real underlying root problem i know that if i just treat the symptoms that we're only treating the symptoms and the root the real root underlying problem is never going to uh is never going to disappear right and they're going to continue to have it so when it comes to the the service that i deliver the guidance that i give the feedback that i give the direction that i give what i teach them what i equip them with it's always going to be what addresses the root underlying problem okay so that's important to understand but when it comes to the marketing i'm going to start with the symptoms because it's the symptoms that most prospects see and believe as their problem see most prospects like i said to you guys over the workshop most prospects don't know the underlying problem right like there are there you take entrepreneurs out in the in the wild marketers folks that aren't as savvy and sophisticated as you guys on here are with copy and they don't know they they've never heard of the big idea they don't know about that many folks don't understand the nuances of a hook they don't know that that's the problem they don't know that the problem is they have no hook right what they know is the symptom that they're dealing with is the result of not having a good hook is the result of not having a good idea the lack of attention they they don't like they might right let's say they may um struggle to generate traffic uh or they may struggle to um to generate sales and they think that they're you know let's say it's because they can't get people to click on their ads and so it must be a traffic they have a a traffic problem it's that they have a hook problem right and and the fact of the matter is that if they had the right hook they would get so you have to understand that when it comes to But as crazy as it sounds, as strange as it sounds, is, you know, you at least not that I can see, you don't use the word webinar on here once. And so right, so you say free training, right free training on here and now I click and I'm in the, I'm almost like, did I get to the right page? Is the link broken? Am I, did this bring me to the right spot? Like is this the same thing that I'm, that he's talking about here? And so congruence, you always like, you know, you got to continue to reassure the visitor, right? Like reassure the visitor, like you got to make sure that you don't do anything that breaks the confidence, breaks the feel of flow. And so, and so I think Damien is spot on with that. The other thing is to just drive home this point with the qualifying. So right, so you know that there is, there's two different types of traffic that you can drive. There is like for, for, you know, to just high level view, right? There is fixed cost and there is performance based, right? There's fixed cost and there's performance based. So performance based is when you pay per visitor, whether you're paying on a CPM, whether you're paying on a cost per click, right? You're paying, in other words, like when you run a Facebook ad, Facebook ads, right, you're paying for a visitor, you're paying to get a click, they're charging you based on a CPM model, but you're paying to get a click ultimately, right? And so that's performance based. And then there is fixed cost where let's say we go and we spend, you know, 5000 bucks and we run a solo ad to, you know, photographers 101.com, their newsletter list of 100,000 people, right? So we go there, we pay them 5000 bucks and now we send out a solo email. Well, the difference between the two is that you qualify performance based traffic. You do not qualify on fixed cost. In other words, I already paid the five grand. Now I'm going to generate as many leads as I can, right? I'm not going to qualify. I'm not going to disqualify. I'm not going to, because there's no, there's no benefit to me in doing that, right? Assuming that, like that they're all photographers and what like, like in other words, we're not qualifying performance, excuse me, we're not qualifying fixed cost. We're qualifying on the performance side. Here, if you're going to qualify, you do it in the ad, not on the landing page because you already paid for it. So why would you want to, why would you want to lose out on that? You already paid for it. The better you get it qualifying, the more, the more it's going to cost you to generate a lead, right? If you disqualify the majority of people, now your lead cost just shot through the roof. And so does that make sense, Jan? Yeah, yeah. Maybe I like to jump in here to help Jan one more time. Can you just turn back to Jan's page for a second? Jan, who is this offer for? Passionate photographers who are probably still working in Microsoft. Okay, so is this for professional photographers? So James asked a question that maybe Teddy can, we'll just, I'll skip to the bottom real, because this is an easy question, tackling question, and Damian, I think Teddy would be the best to answer this because it's a tech thing, but I have a delayed buy button, but it appears straight away that that appears straight away when somebody visits again. I've noticed from screen recordings that many people are getting savvy to this and refresh the page straight away and click through to the checkout page. So Teddy, is there a simple way that you can share with these guys where it's tied to the button appears when they get to a certain part in the video? And so even if they, you know, like if they hit refresh, it won't appear until they get to a certain part in the video. Is there an easy way to talk about that? There's not an easy way. No, it's a requires some JavaScript work to integrate with Wistia, depending on which CRM you're using. I think plus this may have an option to help do it more of a WYSIWYG way, but yeah, we do it with a custom JavaScript, unfortunately, but it sounds like he already has the script working because it is showing the right. It's dropping a cookie and kind of which we do as well, right? So once they've gotten, but for us, it's once they've gotten to the point where the button got showed because they hit that point in the video, then if they come back to the page, then it shows again. Right. And so that's the difference, right? That that's the difference. Once they get to the point in the video, like at the 15 minute and 15 second mark where the button appears, if now they refresh and come back to it, the button will be there. And so if you find that in your market that people, they're hit to it because that's what everybody else is doing. And so they hit the page that no button refresh and now they see the button and then they're just quickly clicking through. And that's what you're seeing from your screen capture recordings. Then yeah, we need to address that, right? Because the, in other words, you need to recognize that the, as the market evolves, remember, right? A known tactic is a blown tactic. And so right, if the market is hip to that, and now you see that, like the majority of people are boom, refresh, there it is, click through, let me see the price, then yeah, I would absolutely address that. And so let me go back now and see, okay, so I said Nadine, so where is Nadine? So Nadine had two questions here. So I'm going to pop this in and I'm going to show you guys the screen. And so let's look at this. So Nadine, number one, I've been working with the concept of feel and grow rich linking to the think and grow rich book by Napoleon Hill. Would that be considered one or two as of the rule of one also since I'm playing with the feeling of it in my copy? Is it specific enough for a primary promise? Okay, so two things. Number one, it depends, right? So number one, right now, there is no, like, what is the promise? Is the promise like, are you saying that the promise is do this and you'll feel and grow rich? If that's the promise, right, do XYZ or, you know, feel and grow rich when you do XYZ? No, I would say that that is not a primary promise because it's not specific, tangible, concrete, right? It's not measurable. It's not measurable. And so I would push you to get more specific in that. The idea is feel and grow rich. Does that go against the rule of one? The answer is no. That doesn't go against the rule of one. As long as what we're talking about is here is a, like, because you're really, here is a way, like the simple formula, like we discussed, like, we're talking about the idea. We discovered a formula in a rare economics textbook that allows you to X and Y. That is one idea, right? That's one idea. We're not talking about how to, we're not saying we discovered something in a textbook that allows you to feel and grow rich and become wildly productive every day with your to-do list. That's a combination of two ideas. Those are two things that are disconnected. You're talking about two completely different things. And so just because you have this feel and grow rich doesn't mean that it's two. That's one idea, and so that's okay. Is there any guidance on where all of those copy chunks we discussed in last part of yesterday's session fit in on a sales letter? So I think that what Nadine is asking here is are the language patterns. And so we discussed, we went through certain language patterns where like somewhere, you know, when we wanted for credibility, like where we would start off, we would say like, you know, all, you know, whatever, you know, almost all the wealthiest people agree, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. We talk about things like, you know, the point for time purposes is wherever in the copy you are trying to prod that feeling, that emotion or that belief, that's where you're going to use the appropriate language pattern. So it's not to say like, do I need to use, do I need to, you know, am I saying that early, am I saying it late? There is no one hard and fast rule of like how early in your copy are you going to say almost all realtors agree, blah, blah, blah, wherever in your copy you are establishing credibility is where you're going to use the language patterns associated with building credibility, wherever you are building report, that's early, which is typically early in the letter, you are building report, you're going to use one of those language patterns. So it's wherever you are stimulating the emotion. So sometimes, right, I might stimulate depending on the theme, depending on who I'm talking to, if I want to stimulate, let's say I'm going much more direct in the in the lead in the theme. I created a back end campaign. And so I'm going to be much more direct. We're leading with the offer. You could see like in the copy workshop thing, I talked about and leverage the fear of missing out very early. It was almost in the deck copy where I talked about warning. There's a certain limited, you know, there's a limited number of seats and once that like, and I'm only doing this once and whatnot. Sometimes right when I'm going much more indirect. Right. And I'm not talking about the I'm going to talk about that. I'm going to stimulate the fear of missing out towards the end, where I'm talking about the actual offer itself. So it's not a matter of is there a is there a rule to where those things get done in a sales letter. There's not like we talked about over the over the weekend. There's a lot of nuance right in depending on who you're talking to depending on how direct you are in the in the promotion. The point is you use those in the places where you are trying to elicit or stimulate the emotion thought or belief that you're aiming for. So G asked, I don't G is still on here. So would you start with a skeleton order form first on the initial MVF? Yes, and that's something that we talk about in E5 coaching. When you roll out an MVF a minimum viable funnel that yes, I would start with a stripped down skeleton order form. We show that any five coaching show what elements to put on there. And so understand that the more you have on there right the more difficult it becomes to understand is the like is the order form the issue because people haven't been prepped to buy or is the order form the issue because there's something on the order form that is distributed. That is dissuading people from buying. And so that's why we start with a skeleton. So we have the least number of things that could cause an issue. Remember like what I said earlier, you know, I think that you know you've got to be careful for me when I when when working with clients, you know clients, they tend to want to put more and more copy like on on the opt in page on the order form. And when when done right like yeah look more copy can be effective but more often than not what happens is the more copy they put on there the more likely they are to take away the curiosity to reveal the mystery to say something that makes the prospect think oh I know what this is now right and so often times I want I want to try to go with the least amount of copy that I can to get the job done right because every additional piece of copy that you include on any page just increasing the number of copies that you put on the order form. And this is your chances of saying something that is going to dissuade somebody right and so that's why honestly when you can get away with it and I've said this before when you have an ad and the ad says, you know how let's take, you know, Jan, you know how any, you know any is Jan still on here. No, Jan is not on here and so we can use somebody else's example back pain whatever for Aaron, you know, like when I say something in an ad in an ideal perfect world what I would love to do is bring them to a landing page. That takes the same headline from the ad and has it on the landing page and has a button period because everything else that when I change things went right. I'm getting somebody to respond to the ad to click on it and come over to the to the opt in form now that that visitor. That's my lead the lose that's how I look at it. That's mine to lose now right and what often happens is people click on an ad because that their interest their interest was peaked. They come over to a landing page and now they see this copy and they see a bullet and it makes them say, oh, I know what he's talking about or it's not what I thought it was or it's different from what what I believe. And so I'd much rather go with a headline and a button that they click to opt in and get access to the thing. And so right like you have to recognize that the more copy you put on there the more elements there are for us to now have to look at and say, could it be this, could it be this, could it be this. Is it the testimonial and now the testimonial makes them feel like it's not appropriate for them. And so start with a skeleton order form. So James asked a question about with everyone scrolling down and being familiar with sales pages what are your thoughts on putting the price on the top in the lead there at the very start of the marketing argument and getting at it and getting it out of the way. So I'll tell you, I'm going to give you my straight answer to that and then I'm going to give you a little nuance. So number one is no, I would not do that why why would I do that. But like, in other words, you know, why would I start off with like, it's almost like I go back to the days when I was in the health club business for many, many years right, and people would call on the phone and they would say, how much is the club, like they would answer the phone team would answer the phone, how much is it, how much is membership. And so we never, never gave pricing out over the phone, why, because if you give price right price absent of value, there's no context to the price, we were higher price than everybody else. Right and so if all we did was give out price over the phone, then the only thing that the person on the other end has to make the decision on is the price. That's expensive. Right. That's a lot more expensive than every other club in the area, but that's how they feel because that is absent of value. That's absent of context. So we would always we would we would we would deflect the question we would say when somebody would say how much is it we would always say, like, is this for you or somebody in your family. Right. Me. Well, this is for somebody, you know, this is for me. Have you ever been into one of our facilities before. No. So what we need to do is set you up with an appointment, show you around and blah blah blah blah blah, show you what we got. And when the person would always come and hawn when the person would say, just, I just want to know how much the prices. We would always say something along the lines of like, you know, is first of all, we need to make sure we want to make sure that the club is right for you but we want to want to, we also want to make sure that you are right for the club we're very selective of who we allow to become members. If price is the only thing that you're concerned with, I could recommend, you know, the typical gym around. There's a lot. There's many, many cheap clubs that you that you could go to. But if you're looking for the best experience, then we need to set you up with an appointment. What's good for you the beginning of the week or the end. And then right, we would go right at we would never give price right. And because if all you do is give price then all your, your giving them to make the decision on his price. I don't want to give them the ability to just make the decision based on price alone. I want to do as much as I can to build the value to give the argument to demonstrate the difference to demonstrate the payoff, which is also by the way, why I like for mid tier and up, I prefer VSL. I like a VSL when you're talking cheap price. Listen to me carefully here right and take this put this to the test. When you're talking a cheap price, like when you're talking, you know, something inexpensive right in it, which is all relative to every market. I'm okay with a long form sales letter and I'm okay knowing that the majority of people are going to scroll down and look at the price. I'm okay with that because I know that the price isn't going to dissuade them. Like when the when, if I brought somebody to a page and they could scroll down and they see that it's $3,000. Well, they're going to scroll down they're going to see it's $3,000. I don't even want to engage with this because I'm not willing to spend $3,000 on anything and so I'm out right but when they scroll down and they see it's 10 bucks or 12 bucks or 20 bucks or whatever that is 30 but 49 bucks or whatever in your in your in your market, whatever it is, then they're like, okay, now I'll now I'll consume right and so when it's a low price I'm cool with them seeing it when it's not when it's mid tier and up. I like VSL because I want to control it right and so I don't want to allow somebody to which is why we don't use we don't give controls on videos. I know people hate it right right you know who are the people that hate it. The people that are hated are the ones that just want to scrub to the end and see the price right and so I don't care that they hate it. I rather than bounce and leave. I don't care. I'm fine with that right because I know that I want them to consume the message like consuming the message. I'm building value. Then when they see the price right I'm trying to eliminate sticker shock. Right you can't eliminate sticker shock if you lead with the price upfront. And so that is my my recommendation. And again, like Josh said here which is smart and this is why there's there's nuance when Josh it you know unless somebody is already product aware. And this, you know, the beauty is, you know, like the beauty really is this is all a learnable skill. Right? This is not like a talent. This is not a talent. It's not like singing. It's not like it's not a talent. These are all learnable skills. There was a time I knew nothing about copy. I didn't know what copy was. I didn't know the difference between marketing and advertising. I thought it was all Coca-Cola big, you know, elephants and yellow things. And I knew nothing. Right? These are all learnable skills. You just got to be willing to learn them, to implement them, learn them, to implement them and continue implementing. And so thank you so much. God bless you guys. I'll see you all soon. In E5, I'll see you guys on the next, the next workshop, which my gosh, I don't even know what the topic is. I don't even know if we've revealed the topic yet, but God bless you guys. Go have an amazing night. I'll see you guys talk soon.
